title: Listening - Part 3: The Mind of a Champion
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a sports psychologist, Dr. Aris Thorne, and a former Olympic swimmer, Elena Varga, about the mental challenges of elite competition. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview exploring how elite athletes handle pressure and the transition to life after sports.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Elena, you’ve stood on the podium at the highest level. Does the pressure ever become routine?

Elena: Never. If anything, it gets heavier. [15]What people don't see is the 'internal monologue' that happens seconds before the whistle—it’s a constant battle to suppress the part of your brain that wants to flee.[/15] You don't eliminate fear; you just learn to coexist with it while your muscles do what they've been trained to do.

Interviewer: Aris, you work with many young athletes. Is 'talent' overrated?

Aris: It’s the starting line, not the finish. [16]I’ve seen dozens of naturally gifted players fail because they lacked 'cognitive flexibility'—the ability to adapt their strategy when a game plan falls apart.[/16] The ones who make it are those who can find a 'Plan B' while their heart rate is at 180 beats per minute.

Interviewer: Elena, you retired at twenty-six. Was that a difficult choice?

Elena: [17]I actually felt a profound sense of relief, which was quite confusing at the time.[/17] I expected to be devastated, but the reality was that I was exhausted by the relentless schedule. The hard part came six months later, when I realized I had no idea who 'Elena' was without a swimming pool.

Interviewer: Aris, is the 'win at all costs' mentality changing?

Aris: Slowly. We are starting to prioritize mental health over pure medals. [18]The real turning point was seeing top-tier athletes speak openly about their vulnerabilities.[/18] It broke the myth that being a champion requires you to be a robot. Now, my sessions are as much about life-balance as they are about visualization techniques.

Interviewer: Elena, what’s your take on the use of data in sports psychology?

Elena: It’s a double-edged sword. Biometrics can tell you if you’re recovered, but [19]I worry that athletes are becoming too reliant on what their watch says instead of listening to their own bodies.[/19] There’s an intuitive 'feel' for performance that you can’t capture in a spreadsheet.

Interviewer: Finally, what is the key to longevity in sports?

Aris: [20]It’s the ability to find joy in the process of training, rather than just the outcome of the race.[/20] If your only happiness comes from the medal, you’re in for a very short and miserable career. Elena, you’ve managed that transition beautifully by coaching the next generation.

questions.question: What does Elena say about the pressure of elite competition?
options: A) It becomes easier to handle with every new race.
B) It involves a mental effort to ignore the instinct to escape.
C) It is completely eliminated by years of physical training.
D) It is less intense than most spectators would imagine.
questions.explanation: She mentions the 'internal monologue' and the battle to 'suppress the part of your brain that wants to flee'.

questions.question: In Aris's opinion, successful athletes are those who
options: A) possess the most natural talent from the beginning.
B) can maintain a heart rate below 180 beats per minute.
C) strictly follow the original game plan without fail.
D) are capable of changing their approach under stress.
questions.explanation: He emphasizes 'cognitive flexibility' and the ability to find a 'Plan B' during the heat of the moment.

questions.question: How did Elena feel immediately after her retirement?
options: A) surprised by the lack of sadness she felt
B) devastated by the loss of her daily routine
C) confused about her identity as a swimmer
D) relieved that she finally had time to go swimming
questions.explanation: She says she felt a 'profound sense of relief' which she found confusing as she expected to be 'devastated'.

questions.question: According to Aris, the public's perception of champions has changed because
options: A) medals are no longer considered important.
B) athletes have shared their personal struggles.
C) robotic technology is now used in most sports.
D) sports psychologists have stopped using visualization.
questions.explanation: He states the turning point was seeing athletes speak 'openly about their vulnerabilities'.

questions.question: Elena’s concern about biometric data is that it
options: A) is not accurate enough to use in spreadsheets.
B) makes athletes ignore their own physical intuition.
C) fails to tell an athlete if they have recovered.
D) is too expensive for most Olympic swimmers.
questions.explanation: She worries athletes are becoming 'too reliant on what their watch says instead of listening to their own bodies'.

questions.question: What does Aris consider essential for a long-lasting career?
options: A) focusing exclusively on winning medals
B) transitioning into coaching as early as possible
C) finding satisfaction in daily preparation
D) ensuring the outcome of the race is always positive
questions.explanation: He says the key is finding 'joy in the process of training, rather than just the outcome'.
